subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Explicit selection of ethics as the key concept, framing the entire investigation around ethical manufacturing and CSR
Strong organization-specific connection demonstrated by discussion of Diorâs Italian subcontractor scandal and stakeholder expectations
Consistent use of ethical considerations to interpret SWOT, stakeholder analysis, and Ansoff Matrix
weaknesses: Ethics is not interwoven in every section; some parts revert to generic strategic language without explicit ethical linkage
Quantitative evidence on Diorâs own metrics is limited, weakening deeper integration of ethics throughout
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows a strong understanding with partially integrated analysis of ethics (Band 4). While ethical considerations frame most analyses and a clear connection to Dior is maintained, the student does not sustain this integration fully in every section and quantitative organizational data is sparse, preventing the highest band.

strengths: Most cited documents are generally relevant to Diorâs supply-chain controversy, Gen Z sustainability demand, and luxury-brand CSR
weaknesses: Nine sources listed, exceeding the recommended 3â5 and resulting in superficial coverage
Documents lack depth, with only brief headline facts extracted and inconsistent academic rigor
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides more than five supporting documents, placing this strand in Band 1. Although relevance is acceptable, the sheer number dilutes depth and fails to meet the criterionâs requirements.

strengths: Appropriate selection of three recognized business tools (SWOT, Stakeholder Mapping, Ansoff Matrix) relevant to the research question
Solid theoretical integration, as seen in the stakeholder vs. shareholder theory discussion
Clear definitions and overviews of each selected framework
weaknesses: No additional financial or CSR-specific metrics to broaden analytical scope
Application remains mostly descriptive and links back to the research question are not always explicit
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates adequate selection and application of tools and theories (Band 3). Tools are relevant and appropriately applied, but the range is limited and application lacks consistently clear linkage to the research question.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Incorporates quantitative data (e.g., Gen Z willingness to pay, Gucci revenue, HBR profitability figures) to support analysis
Effective stakeholder map linking group interests to CSR impacts
Clear paragraphs bridging ethical concepts to profitability outcomes
weaknesses: Absence of Diorâs own sales or financial figures limits relevance
Data citations are sometimes inconsistent (e.g., conflicting percentages)
SWOT analysis lacks quantitative estimates, reducing analytical depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis shows adequate selection and use of data and some analysis of the research question (Band 3). While quantitative support from external sources strengthens the argument, the lack of Dior-specific data and intermittent integration prevents a higher score.

strengths: Provides a clear, direct recommendation explicitly answering the research question
Conclusions align qualitatively with the main analytical threads
weaknesses: Evidence base is largely secondary and thin on Dior-specific data
No implementation timeline or measurable KPIs are specified to support feasibility
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Conclusions are partially consistent with the evidence and explicitly answer the research question, placing them in Band 2. The recommendation is clear, but relies on a thin evidence base and lacks concrete implementation details.

strengths: Logical sequence with clear headings (introduction, tools, conclusion)
Appropriate structure maintained throughout the report
weaknesses: Abrupt transitions between sections (e.g., before Ansoff Matrix)
Some sentences are incomplete or cut off, reducing clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report demonstrates an appropriate overall structure (Band 2), with logical organization, but occasional abrupt transitions and incomplete text detract from overall coherence.

strengths: Includes a title and bibliography as required elements
weaknesses: Missing word count, table of contents, consistent page numbering, and appendices for supporting documents
Inconsistent citation formatting and incomplete entries reduce professionalism
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Key required elements (word count, TOC, page numbers) are missing and citation formatting is inconsistent, placing presentation in Band 1.

strandSelections.reasoning: The report follows a logical sequence (introduction, tool sections, conclusion) with headings and tables, demonstrating an âappropriate structure throughout.â

**Relevance** â Most cited articles deal with Diorâs supply-chain controversy, Gen Z sustainability demand and luxury-brand CSR, so topic relevance is acceptable.  
**Depth** â However, only brief headline facts are extracted; there are no extended excerpts, statistics are sporadic and some sources (e.g., Clastify notes) are merely summary websites. This meets âgenerally relevant but lack depth,â aligning with Band 2.

While a title and bibliography are present, other required elements (word count, table of contents, consistent page numbers, appendices showing supporting documents) are missing. Therefore, âone or more required elementsâ are absent, placing it in Band 1.

The IA references at least nine sources in the bibliography, exceeding the prescribed 3-5 supporting documents. Under rubric guidance, providing more than five documents places the work in Band 1 for this strand.

**Application** â The tools are applied directly to Diorâs situation (e.g., SWOT weakness = ânegative publicity,â Ansoff = âethical product developmentâ).  
**Limitations** â Analysis is mostly descriptive; numerical evidence is sparse and links back to the research question are not always explicit. This matches ârelevance not always clear but adequately applied,â Band 3.

Data such as Gen Z willingness to pay 73 %, Harvard Business Reviewâs 20 % profitability figure and Gucciâs â¬10.5 bn revenue are incorporated. While limited, this shows âadequate selection and use.â Absence of Diorâs own financial data prevents a higher band.

The student links CSR investment to potential revenue gain, weighs short-term cost versus long-term benefit and references stakeholder theory vs. shareholder theory â evidence of âsome analysis of the research question.â Integration is intermittent, so Band 3 is appropriate.
The research question is explicitly answered in the final paragraph with a clear recommendation, fulfilling the Band 3 descriptor of an explicit answer.

Three recognised BM tools (SWOT, Stakeholder Mapping, Ansoff Matrix) are selected and appropriate to the question, satisfying an âadequateâ rather than âlimitedâ range. No financial ratio or CSR-specific metrics are added, so the work does not reach the âeffectiveâ breadth of Band 4.

The conclusion that Dior *should* pursue ethical manufacturing is broadly aligned with the qualitative arguments presented, but the supporting evidence base is thin and largely secondary. This partial alignment matches Band 2 (âsome conclusions consistent with evidenceâ).

**Strengths** â The student refers directly to Diorâs recent Italian subcontractor scandal, cites financial stability, reputational risk and specific Gen Z expectations, and contrasts Dior with competitors such as Gucci and Prada. This shows a strong, organisation-specific focus.  
**Weaknesses** â Quantitative evidence on Dior itself is thin (no revenue figures, limited financial trend data), so the integration is not fully comprehensive.  
The analysis is therefore âstrong â¦ with partial integration throughout,â placing it in Band 4.

**Strengths** â The student explicitly selects ethics as the key concept and repeatedly frames the investigation around âethical manufacturingâ and CSR. Ethical considerations are used to interpret the SWOT, stakeholder analysis and Ansoff Matrix, showing sound conceptual grasp.  
**Weaknesses** â Discussion of ethics is not interwoven through *every* section; parts of the SWOT and Ansoff commentary slip back into generic strategy language without explicit ethical linkage.  
Given this solid but not fully pervasive integration, the work best fits the descriptor âstrong understanding with partially integrated analysis,â corresponding to Band 4.

annotations.comment: The discussion of stakeholder vs. shareholder theory demonstrates solid theoretical integration.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The student provides a concise overview of selected tools (SWOT, Stakeholder, Ansoff), demonstrating an appropriate selection of frameworks.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The introduction to stakeholder analysis succinctly defines the toolâs purpose and relevance to the research question.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated but could be refined for conciseness and grammatical correctness to enhance clarity.
The student explicitly identifies âethicsâ as the key concept, setting a clear conceptual focus for the analysis.
The introduction outlines the use of secondary sources and defines the scope, but it should acknowledge limitations and potential biases of relying solely on public reports.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The methodology describes competitor benchmarks and Gen Z data, but lacks Diorâs own sales or revenue figures to strengthen relevance.
The SWOT summary paragraph lacks explicit reference to ethical implications in each quadrant; integrate ethics language directly into the discussion.
The SWOT table identifies key factors but omits any quantitative estimates (e.g., cost of supply chain overhaul) that would deepen the analysis.
The Ansoff table merges quadrants inaccurately, making it hard to distinguish Market Development vs. Diversification strategies.
The Ansoff analysis would benefit from a specific Dior product example, illustrating how ethical lines could be developed.
The paragraph linking ethics to consumer impacts effectively bridges theoretical concepts and profitability outcomes.
The stakeholder map covers all relevant groups and links their interests to CSR impacts, demonstrating effective application of this tool.
The closing sentence is cut off midâthought (âinvestors may resist shortâtermâ), creating an incomplete analysis and reducing clarity.
The transitional paragraph before Ansoff is abrupt; consider linking insights from stakeholder risks directly to strategic matrix choices.
The Ansoff Matrix is defined clearly, showing the studentâs understanding of growth strategies in context.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The inclusion of Gen Z willingness-to-pay statistics (67 %) is effective, but earlier the student cited 73 %; ensure data consistency.
The Gucci revenue figure (â¬10.5 bn) adds persuasive quantitative support for linking ethics to profitability gains.
The discussion of Diorâs Italian subcontractor scandal demonstrates strong connection to the organizationâs current ethical challenges.
Citing a Harvard Business Review report with a 20 % profitability increase strengthens the argument with a reputable source.
The synthesizing paragraph reiterates points without adding new insights; consider deeper evaluation rather than summary.
The conclusion provides a clear, direct recommendation, explicitly answering the research question.
The conclusion could be strengthened by specifying an implementation timeline and measurable KPIs for ethical initiatives.
The bibliography lists nine documents, exceeding the recommended 3â5 and diluting depth; trimming to the most relevant 5 would improve focus.
Citation entries vary in completeness and format; for example, entry 4 lacks a full URL and access date, reducing professionalism.
Ensure all online sources include DOI or access dates consistently to enhance academic rigor and traceability.
